<!-- question-type: inclass -->
### Exercise 6 [Optional, if time]: Improving the Listings by Region Plot

**(a).** Together with your peers, propose two changes you want to make to the plot in Exercise 1. Discuss the rationale behind each change and how it might improve the visualization.

**(b).** Work with your tutor to create an agreed upon list of changes to make to the plot. What steps do you need to take to make these improvements?

**(c).** Implement the changes suggested in the R.

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.teaching-block}

::: {.teaching-block-header}
Teaching Note
:::

::: {.teaching-block-body}

🎯 **Learning Objective** 
Students should:

- Evaluate and improve a basic bar plot using best practices in data visualization
- **ADVANCED**: Implement proportional comparisons, reordering of categories
- **ADVANCED**: Deepen their understanding of ggplot2 internals, especially `after_stat()` behavior in `geom_bar()`

✅   **Core Concepts to Highlight**

- Reordering bars using `fct_infreq()` displays categories from most to least common, making comparisons intuitive
- **ADVANCED**: Proportions on the y-axis improve interpretability, especially when raw counts vary in scale — but require extra care to calculate within `geom_bar()`
- **ADVANCED**: `after_stat(count)` lets us access the default computed statistic (the count of rows)
- **ADVANCED**: `after_stat(count) / sum(after_stat(count)`) computes the proportion of each bar relative to the entire set of visible bars, not the whole dataset — this line is non-obvious and worth unpacking live

💬 **Suggested In-Class Prompts** (if needed)

“Why might we want to show proportions instead of counts?”

“What would happen if we didn’t use `after_stat()` here? What warning might we see?”

“Does this plot make you think differently about which regions are most active?”

“When might we want to show counts instead of proportions?”

📌 **Common Misunderstandings**

- Thinking `fct_infreq()` changes the data itself — it only affects display order, not values
- Misusing `stat = "identity"` in `geom_bar()` instead of working with the default stat (count) — in this context, we want `stat = "count"` (the default), then adjust the output with `after_stat()`

:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-solutions -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-solutions" when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.solution-block}

::: {.solution-block-header}
Solution
:::

**(a) and (b)**

Here the biggest improvement is analytical rather than technical

Analytical changes

* ordering bars by frequency
* showing proportions instead of counts

Presentation changes

* colored fill
* percent labels
* rotated axis text
* theme_bw()
* centered title

**(c)**

::: {.solution-block-body}

```{r}
# Solution:  use proportions, order bars, and center title
region_bar <- 
    # 2. Create the plot
    ggplot(housing,
        # 2a. Reorder the region names based on frequency
        aes(x = fct_infreq(regionname))
    ) +
    # 3. Plot bar heights as proportions, not raw counts
    geom_bar(
      # THIS BIT IS HARD
      # YOU CAN NOT USE IT AND KEEP Y AXIS AS COUNTS
        aes(y = after_stat(count) / sum(after_stat(count))),
        fill = "grey"
    ) +
    # 4. Add informative axis labels and a title
    labs(
        x = "Region", 
        y = "Proportion of Listings",  # Note: this is technically a proportion, not % format
        title = "Number of Listings by Region"
    ) +
  # 5. Tweak the theme for better readability and presentation
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
    theme_bw() +
    theme(
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = 1),  # Rotate x labels
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5)              # Center the title
    )

region_bar
```


:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-solutions -->
